name: Area Near Jenson Quarry
short_description: BLM public land near Jurupa Valley offering casual rockhounding for quartz, feldspar, and calcite specimens. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
description:
The area near Jenson Quarry in Riverside County sits on Bureau of Land Management public land, making it accessible for recreational rockhounding without permits or fees. The site sits at 1,180 feet elevation on gently sloping terrain typical of the inland Riverside County landscape, where Mesozoic granitic and sedimentary formations have weathered to expose a variety of minerals in surface deposits and dry washes.

This location is known for collectors seeking quartz specimens, feldspar fragments, and calcite deposits that weather out of the surrounding bedrock. The gentle slopes and accessible terrain make this a good option for beginners and families new to rockhounding. The dry washes and sandy areas are the primary collecting zones where minerals concentrate naturally.

Rockhounders should expect warm, dry conditions typical of inland Southern California. Bring plenty of water and sun protection. The site is relatively close to urban services in Jurupa Valley, making it convenient for half-day collecting trips. Always respect BLM regulations: collect for personal use only, do not exceed 25 pounds per day, and avoid any active mining operations or marked restricted areas.

The granite-derived soils and exposed outcrops in this region have attracted casual collectors for decades. This area represents typical Inland Empire geology where uplift and erosion have exposed mineral-rich parent rocks.
history: This area underlies the western Transverse Ranges, where Mesozoic granitic intrusions and older metamorphic rocks form the bedrock. Quaternary alluvial deposits blanket the slopes, constantly weathering and exposing new mineral specimens. The Jenson Quarry itself marks a long history of aggregate and stone extraction in this region, reflecting the mineral-rich character of the local geology.
tips: Search in dry washes and sandy areas where water erosion has concentrated lighter minerals - quartz and feldspar fragments are easiest to spot here. Bring a small hand lens and rock hammer to test hardness and identify feldspars; look for glassy luster and light coloring (white, pink, or tan). Focus your efforts on the base of gentle slopes where runoff naturally deposits heavier material, and collect in early morning before heat becomes intense.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,180 feet with gentle slopes characteristic of the inland Riverside Valley. Terrain consists of sandy washes, low hills, and scattered outcrops with sparse desert shrub vegetation. Walking distance from parking to productive areas is typically 0.25 to 0.5 miles. Footing is generally stable on sandy terrain but watch for loose rocks and scattered boulders.
best_season: October - April. The low elevation and inland location experience intense summer heat (often exceeding 95F from May through September). Winter and spring offer comfortable collecting conditions with temperatures in the 60s-75F range, though December - February can occasionally dip below freezing at dawn.
written_directions: From Jurupa Valley, head east on 6th Street toward the unincorporated Riverside County areas. The Jenson Quarry area is approximately 3-4 miles northeast of central Jurupa Valley. Access is via local roads through the BLM-managed lands. Use GPS coordinates 34.025681, -117.433226 to locate the public collecting area. Approach via passenger vehicle on paved and unpaved local roads; confirm current access at the Riverside County BLM field office.
road_conditions: Mix of paved and unpaved local roads. Some washboard surfaces possible on BLM access roads. High-clearance vehicles recommended but standard passenger cars can access most areas.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas scattered throughout the BLM land near Jenson Quarry. No developed parking lot. Multiple areas suitable for day-use parking with space for 2-4 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect all marked restricted areas and avoid active mining operations.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or digging equipment
Avoid marked restricted areas
No collecting within active quarry boundaries
nearest_services: Jurupa Valley, approximately 3-4 miles southwest. Gas stations, restaurants, and general stores available in town.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved roads and sandy washes require sturdy footwear but no rock scrambling needed. Terrain suitable for ages 6 and up with supervision. Not wheelchair accessible due to unpaved roads and wash terrain.

faq.question: What can you find rockhounding near Jenson Quarry in Riverside County?
faq.answer: The area near Jenson Quarry is known for quartz specimens, feldspar fragments, and calcite deposits. Collectors find mostly white and clear quartz pieces, light-colored feldspars (white to pink), and occasional calcite crystals in the sandy washes. Specimens range from small fragments to hand-sized pieces suitable for collections.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at the Jenson Quarry area in California?
faq.answer: No permit is required. The area sits on BLM public land where personal use rockhounding is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You only need to follow the 25 lb per day collection limit and avoid commercial use. No fees apply.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect rocks near Jurupa Valley?
faq.answer: October through April offers the best collecting conditions. Summer temperatures exceed 95F making fieldwork uncomfortable, while winter months (Dec-Feb) can be cool but are still workable. Spring (March-April) provides ideal temperatures in the 65-75F range with minimal rain.

faq.question: Can families with young children rockhound at the Jenson Quarry area?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a beginner-friendly site suitable for families with children ages 6 and up. The gentle terrain, short walking distances (typically under half a mile), and easy-to-spot minerals make it ideal for introducing kids to rockhounding. Bring plenty of water and sun protection for young collectors.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding near Jenson Quarry?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand lens, basic rock hammer, and collection bag for the 25 lb daily limit. Most importantly, carry plenty of water (2-3 liters per person), sunscreen, and a hat for the exposed terrain. A GPS unit or smartphone with offline maps helps you locate the exact coordinates and return to productive areas.

faq.question: Is the Jenson Quarry rockhounding area dog-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on BLM land for personal use rockhounding. Keep your dog under control and bring extra water for them in the hot months. The sandy terrain and gentle slopes are comfortable for dogs, though paw pads can get hot in summer heat.
